title: What is the future for energy storage? — The Limits of Battery Performance and the Interplay of Chemistry, Materials Science, and Scalability
domain: Energy Storage / Lithium-ion Batteries / Post-Lithium Systems / Materials Science / Electrochemistry / Grid-Scale Storage / Raw Material Supply Chain / Battery Recycling / Techno-Economic Analysis
objective: 围绕一个可证伪的科学问题：'电极材料（如钴酸锂、磷酸铁锂、硫正极）的固有化学稳定性、电解液分解电位、锂/钠离子迁移动力学以及固态电解质界面（SEI）的演化，是否共同决定了一个明确的能量密度上限（例如 < 500 Wh/kg）和循环寿命阈值（例如 < 10,000次，在80%容量保持率下），从而在技术和经济可行性上构成固态/液态电池的固有极限；该上限是否可以通过全新的化学体系（如锂-硫、锂-空气、钠离子）或梯度浓度/纳米结构工程被实质性突破？'

papergraph.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Zehao Cui et al. (2025) A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries doi:10.1002/adma.202420463
papergraph.abstract: Lithium‐ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely adopted in the automotive industry, with an annual global production exceeding 1000 GWh. Despite their success, the escalating demand for LIBs has created concerns on supply chain issues related to key elements, such as lithium, cobalt, and nickel. Sodium‐ion batteries (SIBs) are emerging as a promising alternative due to the high abundance and low cost of sodium and other raw materials. Nevertheless, the commercialization of SIBs, particularly for grid storage and automotive applications, faces significant hurdles. This perspective article aims to identify the critical challenges in making SIBs viable from both chemical and techno‐economic perspectives. First, a brief comparison of the materials chemistry, working mechanisms, and cost between mainstream LIB systems and prospective SIB systems is provided. The intrinsic challenges of SIBs regarding storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, calendar life, and safe operation of cathode, electrolyte, and anode materials are discussed. Furthermore, issues related to the scalability of material production, materials engineering feasibility, and energy‐dense electrode design and fabrication are illustrated. Finally, promising pathways are listed and discussed toward achieving high‐energy‐density, stable, cost‐effective SIBs.

papergraph.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics
papergraph.citation: Wenhua Zuo et al. (2023) Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics doi:10.1021/acs.accounts.2c00690
papergraph.abstract: Conspectus Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are ubiquitous in all modern portable electronic devices such as mobile phones and laptops as well as for powering hybrid electric vehicles and other large-scale devices. Sodium-ion batteries (NIBs), which possess a similar cell configuration and working mechanism, have already been proven as ideal alternatives for large-scale energy storage systems. The advantages of NIBs are as follows. First, sodium resources are abundantly distributed in the earth’s crust. Second, high-performance NIB cathode materials can be fabricated by using solely inexpensive and noncritical transition metals such as manganese and iron, which further reduces the cost of the required raw materials. Recently, the unprecedented demand for lithium and other critical minerals has driven the cost of these primary raw materials (which are utilized in LIBs) to a historic high and thus triggered the commercialization of NIBs. Sodium layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2, TM = transition metal/s), such as Mn-based sodium layered oxides, represent an important family of cathode materials with the potential to reduce costs, increase energy density and cycling stability, and improve the safety of NIBs for large-scale energy storage. However, these layered oxides face several key challenges, including irreversible phase transformations during cycling, poor air stability, complex charge-compensation mechanisms, and relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs. Our work has focused on the techno-economic analysis, the degradation mechanism of NaxTMO2 upon cycling and air exposure, and the development of effective strategies to improve their electrochemical performances and air stability. Correlating structure–performance relationships and establishing general design strategies of NaxTMO2 must be considered for the commercialization of NIBs. In this Account, we discuss the recent progress in the development of air-stable, electrochemically stable, and cost-effective NaxTMO2. The favorable redox-active cations for NaxTMO2 are emphasized in terms of abundance, cost, supply, and energy density. Different working mechanisms related to NaxTMO2 are summarized, including the electrochemical reversibility, the main structural transformations during the charge and discharge processes, and the charge-compensation mechanisms that accompany the (de)intercalation of Na+ ions, followed by discussions to improve the stability toward ambient air and upon cycling. Then the techno-economics are presented, with an emphasis on cathodes with different chemical compositions, cost breakdown of battery packs, and Na deficiency, factors that are critical to the large-scale implementation. Finally, this Account concludes with an overview of the remaining challenges and new opportunities concerning the practical applications of NaxTMO2, with an emphasis on the cost, large-scale fabrication capability, and electrochemical performance.

papergraph.title: Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass
papergraph.citation: G. Perkins (2018) Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass doi:10.1016/J.ENCONMAN.2018.06.090
papergraph.abstract: The strong growth of intermittent electricity generation from solar PV and wind is leading to a greater need for energy storage at grid scale. In this work a techno-economic model has been constructed to calculate the levelised cost of electricity for two systems that can meet an arbitrary energy demand curve: (1) solar PV and battery storage and (2) solar PV with combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas from biomass. The analysis is performed for conditions prevalent in Queensland, Australia where over a gigawatt of new solar PV capacity is being constructed in 2018. The battery storage assumes lithium-ion batteries and costs derived from the recently constructed Homsdale Power Reserve in South Australia. A variable energy demand curve is assumed in the work. The model shows that the parameters with the most impact on the LCOE for the solar PV and battery system are the solar yield, and total installed costs of the battery and solar PV unit. Assuming, battery costs of 750 AUD/kM/b., solar PV costs of 1.6 AUD/W and a project capacity of 240 MWh/d, the LCOE of the solar PV and battery system was calculated to be 170 AUD/MWh. Using total installed costs forecast for the near future, the LCOE is expected to be in the range 150-185 AUD/W for the variable energy demand curve, and over 200 AUD/MWh if a constant supply of power is required. The parameters with the most impact on the LCOE for the solar PV and bio-crude system are the solar yield and total installed cost of the biomass pyrolysis and bio-crude combustion unit. For a 240 MWh/d project scale with variable energy demand, the LCOE for the solar PV and bio-crude system is estimated to be 116 AUD/MWh. Variations in feedstock cost and project scale showed that the LCOE is in the range of 104-125 AUD/MWh. The main conclusion from this work, is that integration of solar PV and the production and combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas using fast pyrolysis of biomass, leads to competitively priced dispatchable renewable energy that is forecast to be cheaper than using solar PV and batteries for the foreseeable future. It has also been found that the combination of solar PV and bio-crude combustion leads to lower LCOEs than using bioenergy alone, due to the rapidly decreasing costs of large scale solar PV. While the solar PV and bio-crude system analysed in this work will likely be a niche solution, in areas with substantial biomass resources, it offers a credible starting point for the development of larger scale bioenergy value chains, with the longer term goal of converting lignocellulosic biomass materials into renewable transportation fuels and chemicals.
papergraph.contribution: integration of solar PV and the production and combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas using fast pyrolysis of biomass leads to competitively priced dispatchable renewable energy, forecast to be cheaper than using solar PV and batteries for the foreseeable future
papergraph.limitation: the solar PV and bio-crude system analysed in this work will likely be a niche solution, in areas with substantial biomass resources

papergraph.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications
papergraph.citation: M. Farhan et al. (2025) Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications doi:10.36347/sjpms.2025.v12i05.005
papergraph.venue: Scholars Journal of Physics Mathematics and Statistics
papergraph.abstract: The need for effective, scalable, and sustainable energy storage solutions has increased due to the quick spread of electric cars, portable gadgets, and renewable energy sources. Because of their extended cycle life, high energy density, and established manufacturing infrastructure, lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have long dominated the energy storage market. However, the hunt for substitute technologies, especially sodium-ion (Na-ion) batteries, which take advantage of the cheap and plentiful sodium, has accelerated due to the limited supply and growing expense of lithium resources. This paper thoroughly analyzes Li-ion and Na-ion electrochemical systems, emphasizing the fundamental ideas, current developments, and new difficulties related to these technologies. Important elements, including electrolytes, separators, cathode and anode materials, and electrode/electrolyte interactions, are thoroughly investigated. Innovative material advancements that improve battery performance and safety are highlighted, such as layered oxide cathodes, alloy and conversion-type anodes, solid-state and gel polymer electrolytes, and surface modification methods. The energy density, rate capability, cycle stability, environmental effect, and cost-efficiency of Li-ion and Na-ion systems are compared. The analysis also examines the commercialization and scaling prospects of next-generation batteries, emphasizing initiatives in recycling, green synthesis, and smart grid integration. The environmental and techno-economic effects of switching from lithium to sodium-based chemistry are specifically discussed. This review's main goals are to summarize existing knowledge, pinpoint technological gaps, and delineate future research goals that might propel the creation of sustainable, high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems suited for a variety of cutting-edge applications.

papergraph.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Jiajun Chen et al. (2025) Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries doi:10.1149/ma2025-023374mtgabs
papergraph.abstract: The transition to sustainable energy storage systems necessitates the development of cathode materials that combine high performance with resource earth-abundance. Manganese-rich layered oxides have emerged as promising candidates, offering high specific capacities and favorable sustainability. This presentation will discuss work ongoing at Argonne National Laboratory related to the study of lithium-ion cathodes. In this work, we report the design and electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions tailored. Through systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess, alongside modifications, we achieve stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations. Selected compositions deliver high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages, demonstrating their suitability for EVs and grid-scale applications. Techno-economic analysis further supports the commercial potential of these materials by projecting competitiveness with commercial cathode materials. These findings underscore the viability of Mn-based layered oxides as next-generation cathodes that address both performance and supply chain resilience.
papergraph.contribution: design and electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions with improved performance and commercial potential

papergraph.title: Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools
papergraph.citation: N. Kebir et al. (2023) Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools doi:10.1038/s41598-023-28377-7
papergraph.abstract: As the world transitions to net zero, energy storage is becoming increasingly important for applications such as electric vehicles, mini-grids, and utility-scale grid stability. The growing demand for storage will constrain raw battery materials, reduce the availability of new batteries, and increase the rate of battery retirement. As retired batteries are difficult to recycle into components, to avoid huge amounts of battery waste, reuse and repurposing options are needed. In this research, we explore the feasibility of using second-life batteries (which have been retired from their first intended life) and solar photovoltaics to provide affordable energy access to primary schools in Kenya. Based on interviews with 12 East African schools, realistic system sizes were determined with varying solar photovoltaic sizes (5–10 kW in 2.5 kW increments) and lithium-ion battery capacities (5–20 kWh in 5 kWh increments). Each combination was simulated under four scenarios as a sensitivity analysis of battery transportation costs (i.e., whether they are sourced locally or imported). A techno-economic analysis is undertaken to compare new and second-life batteries in the resulting 48 system scenarios in terms of cost and performance. We find that second-life batteries decrease the levelized cost of electricity by 5.6–35.3% in 97.2% of scenarios compared to similar systems with new batteries, and by 41.9–64.5% compared to the cost of the same energy service provided by the utility grid. The systems with the smallest levelized cost of electricity (i.e., 0.11 USD/kWh) use either 7.5 kW or 10 kW of solar with 20 kWh of storage. Across all cases, the payback period is decreased by 8.2–42.9% using second-life batteries compared to new batteries; the system with the smallest payback period (i.e., 2.9 years) uses 5 kW solar and 5 kWh storage. These results show second-life batteries to be viable and cost-competitive compared to new batteries for school electrification in Kenya, providing the same benefits while reducing waste.
papergraph.scenario: affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools
papergraph.benchmark: levelized cost of electricity | payback period
papergraph.contribution: second-life batteries decrease the levelized cost of electricity and payback period, making them viable and cost-competitive for school electrification in Kenya
papergraph.limitation: Each combination was simulated under four scenarios as a sensitivity analysis of battery transportation costs (i.e., whether they are sourced locally or imported).

keynotes.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries

keynote.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The escalating demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has created supply chain issues, and there is a need to explore sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) as a viable alternative, especially for grid storage and automotive applications. However, SIBs face significant challenges in terms of performance, stability, and cost-effectiveness.
contributions: Identifies the critical challenges in making SIBs commercially viable from both chemical and techno-economic perspectives.
Provides a comparison between mainstream LIB systems and prospective SIB systems in terms of materials chemistry, working mechanisms, and cost.
Discusses the intrinsic challenges of SIBs including storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, calendar life, and safe operation of cathode, electrolyte, and anode materials.
Illustrates issues related to the scalability of material production, materials engineering feasibility, and energy-dense electrode design and fabrication.

gap_signals.text: Cost-effectiveness and scalability of SIBs compared to LIBs are areas that require further research and development.
Significant hurdles in the commercialization of SIBs, particularly in grid storage and automotive applications, need to be addressed.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are emerging as a promising alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) due to the high abundance and low cost of sodium and other raw materials.
important_claims.evidence: The paper discusses the potential of SIBs as an alternative to LIBs, highlighting the advantages of using abundant and low-cost materials.

important_claims.claim: The commercialization of SIBs faces significant challenges, including storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, and safe operation.
important_claims.evidence: The paper provides a detailed discussion on the intrinsic challenges of SIBs, emphasizing the need to address these issues for successful commercialization.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This perspective article provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions for sodium-ion battery technology, which can serve as a valuable resource for researchers and industry professionals looking to advance the field.

keynotes.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics

keynote.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics
keynote.core_problem: Sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) with layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2) face challenges such as irreversible phase transformations during cycling, poor air stability, complex charge-compensation mechanisms, and relatively high cost compared to LiFePO4-based lithium-ion batteries (LIBs).
contributions: Techno-economic analysis of NaxTMO2 cathodes for NIBs
Investigation into the degradation mechanism of NaxTMO2 upon cycling and air exposure
Development of strategies to improve electrochemical performance and air stability of NaxTMO2
limitations: Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs

gap_signals.text: Complex charge-compensation mechanisms
Irreversible phase transformations during cycling
Poor air stability
Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs
Remaining challenges and new opportunities concerning the practical applications of NaxTMO2, with an emphasis on cost, large-scale fabrication capability, and electrochemical performance.

important_claims.claim: Sodium resources are abundantly distributed in the earth’s crust, making them a viable alternative to lithium.
important_claims.evidence: Sodium is more abundant and can be sourced using inexpensive and noncritical transition metals like manganese and iron, reducing the cost of raw materials.

important_claims.claim: High-performance NIB cathode materials can reduce costs, increase energy density, and improve safety for large-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: NaxTMO2, such as Mn-based sodium layered oxides, have the potential to address these needs, despite current limitations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions for NaxTMO2 cathodes, which can guide further research and development in improving their stability, cost, and performance for practical applications in NIBs.

keynotes.title: Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass

keynote.title: Techno-economic comparison of the levelised cost of electricity generation from solar PV and battery storage with solar PV and combustion of bio-crude using fast pyrolysis of biomass
keynote.core_problem: The growth of intermittent electricity generation from solar PV and wind is increasing the need for energy storage at grid scale, leading to a comparison of different systems that can meet an arbitrary energy demand curve.
contributions: A techno-economic model to calculate the levelised cost of electricity (LCOE) for two systems: (1) solar PV and battery storage, and (2) solar PV with combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas from biomass.
methods: Construction of a techno-economic model to analyze LCOE under conditions prevalent in Queensland, Australia.
Assumption of a variable energy demand curve and use of specific costs (e.g., lithium-ion batteries, solar PV).
experiments_or_evidence: Calculation of LCOE for a 240 MWh/d project capacity for both systems.
Analysis of the impact of varying parameters such as solar yield, installed costs, and feedstock costs on LCOE.
assumptions: Use of lithium-ion batteries and costs derived from the Homsdale Power Reserve in South Australia.
Specific costs for battery (750 AUD/kM/b.) and solar PV (1.6 AUD/W).
limitations: The analysis is specific to the conditions in Queensland, Australia, and may not be directly applicable to other regions.
The solar PV and bio-crude system is considered a niche solution, primarily useful in areas with substantial biomass resources.

gap_signals.text: The solar PV and bio-crude system is considered a niche solution, primarily useful in areas with substantial biomass resources.
The analysis is specific to the conditions in Queensland, Australia, and may not be directly applicable to other regions.
Development of larger scale bioenergy value chains and conversion of lignocellulosic biomass into renewable transportation fuels and chemicals.

datasets_or_materials: Energy demand data for Queensland, Australia.
Cost data for lithium-ion batteries and solar PV.
code_or_implementation: No specific code or implementation details provided.

important_claims.claim: The LCOE for the solar PV and battery system was calculated to be 170 AUD/MWh, with future estimates in the range of 150-185 AUD/MWh for variable energy demand and over 200 AUD/MWh for constant supply.
important_claims.evidence: Modeling results based on specified costs and conditions in Queensland, Australia.

important_claims.claim: The LCOE for the solar PV and bio-crude system is estimated to be 116 AUD/MWh, with variations in feedstock cost and project scale showing a range of 104-125 AUD/MWh.
important_claims.evidence: Modeling results based on specified costs and conditions in Queensland, Australia.

important_claims.claim: Integration of solar PV and the production and combustion of bio-crude and bio-gas using fast pyrolysis of biomass leads to competitively priced dispatchable renewable energy, forecast to be cheaper than using solar PV and batteries for the foreseeable future.
important_claims.evidence: Comparative LCOE analysis between the two systems.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The techno-economic model and LCOE calculations provide a valuable framework for comparing different energy storage and generation systems, particularly in regions with significant biomass resources. The findings can guide future research and development in integrated renewable energy solutions.

keynotes.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications

keynote.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications
keynote.core_problem: The need for effective, scalable, and sustainable energy storage solutions has increased due to the quick spread of electric cars, portable gadgets, and renewable energy sources. The limited supply and growing expense of lithium resources have accelerated the search for alternative technologies, such as sodium-ion (Na-ion) batteries.
contributions: Thorough analysis of Li-ion and Na-ion electrochemical systems, including fundamental concepts, current developments, and new challenges.
Comparison of energy density, rate capability, cycle stability, environmental impact, and cost-efficiency between Li-ion and Na-ion systems.
Discussion on the commercialization and scaling prospects of next-generation batteries, focusing on recycling, green synthesis, and smart grid integration.
Evaluation of the environmental and techno-economic effects of switching from lithium to sodium-based chemistry.

gap_signals.text: Pinpointing technological gaps in the development of Li-ion and Na-ion battery systems.
Delineating future research goals that could advance the creation of sustainable, high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems.

important_claims.claim: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have long dominated the energy storage market due to their extended cycle life, high energy density, and established manufacturing infrastructure.
Sodium-ion (Na-ion) batteries are emerging as a promising alternative due to the cheap and plentiful nature of sodium.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of both Li-ion and Na-ion battery technologies, highlighting key advancements and areas for future research, which can be valuable for researchers and industry professionals looking to develop or improve energy storage solutions.

keynotes.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The need for high-performance, resource-abundant cathode materials for sustainable energy storage systems, particularly for EVs and grid-scale applications.
contributions: Design and electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions
Achievement of stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations through systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess
Demonstration of high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages
Techno-economic analysis supporting the commercial potential of these materials
methods: Systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess
Electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions
experiments_or_evidence: Stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations
High-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages

gap_signals.text: Details on the specific modifications and their impact on the performance are not provided.

important_claims.claim: Mn-rich layered oxides deliver high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages, making them suitable for EVs and grid-scale applications.
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical evaluation shows stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations.

important_claims.claim: Techno-economic analysis supports the commercial potential of Mn-based layered oxides, projecting competitiveness with commercial cathode materials.
important_claims.evidence: The analysis indicates that these materials can be competitive in terms of cost and performance.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The methods and findings provide a foundation for further research into the optimization and commercialization of Mn-rich layered oxide cathodes, potentially leading to more sustainable and high-performance battery technologies.

keynotes.title: Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools

keynote.title: Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools
keynote.core_problem: The growing demand for energy storage, driven by the transition to net zero, is leading to constraints on raw battery materials and an increase in retired batteries. This necessitates the exploration of reuse and repurposing options to avoid waste and provide affordable energy access, particularly in contexts like primary schools in Kenya.
contributions: Feasibility study of using second-life batteries and solar photovoltaics for energy access in Kenyan primary schools
Techno-economic analysis comparing new and second-life batteries in 48 system scenarios
Identification of cost savings and performance benefits of using second-life batteries
methods: Interviews with 12 East African schools to determine realistic system sizes
Simulation of 48 system scenarios with varying solar PV sizes (5–10 kW) and lithium-ion battery capacities (5–20 kWh)
Sensitivity analysis of battery transportation costs (local vs. imported)
experiments_or_evidence: Simulation results showing that second-life batteries decrease the levelized cost of electricity by 5.6–35.3% in 97.2% of scenarios compared to new batteries
Cost reduction of 41.9–64.5% compared to the utility grid
Payback period decreased by 8.2–42.9% using second-life batteries compared to new batteries
assumptions: Batteries are sourced either locally or imported, affecting transportation costs
System sizes and configurations are based on interviews with 12 East African schools

gap_signals.text: Further studies could explore the long-term reliability and maintenance requirements of second-life battery systems in similar settings.

datasets_or_materials: Data from interviews with 12 East African schools

important_claims.claim: Second-life batteries decrease the levelized cost of electricity by 5.6–35.3% in 97.2% of scenarios compared to new batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation results from 48 system scenarios with varying solar PV sizes and battery capacities.

important_claims.claim: Systems with the smallest levelized cost of electricity (i.e., 0.11 USD/kWh) use either 7.5 kW or 10 kW of solar with 20 kWh of storage.
important_claims.evidence: Results from the techno-economic analysis of the 48 system scenarios.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This research provides a framework for evaluating the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of second-life battery systems, which can be adapted for other regions and applications where affordable energy access is needed.

boxue_delegation_plans.goal: 围绕一个可证伪的科学问题：'电极材料（如钴酸锂、磷酸铁锂、硫正极）的固有化学稳定性、电解液分解电位、锂/钠离子迁移动力学以及固态电解质界面（SEI）的演化，是否共同决定了一个明确的能量密度上限（例如 < 500 Wh/kg）和循环寿命阈值（例如 < 10,000次，在80%容量保持率下），从而在技术和经济可行性上构成固态/液态电池的固有极限；该上限是否可以通过全新的化学体系（如锂-硫、锂-空气、钠离子）或梯度浓度/纳米结构工程被实质性突破？'

comparison.prompt_assign_task: implemented as persistent create_task DAG entries with assigned agent, phase, dependency, deliverable, priority, and acceptance criteria
comparison.prompt_review_output: implemented as reviewer_gate plus boxue_final dependency gates; Boxue can add revision tasks via the task system
comparison.prompt_synthesize: implemented as bianlun_synthesis, paperwriter_draft, and boxue_final synthesis tasks
comparison.prompt_adjust_plan: implemented operationally by creating additional tasks or new delegation plans after reviewing outputs
comparison.prompt_finalize: implemented as boxue_final decision task, not as specialist execution

stronger_than_old_pipeline_tasks: role-specific tasks instead of generic phase tasks
risk constraints embedded in every task
optional teammate spawning for unblocked specialist work
prompt_alignment.remaining_manual_gate: Boxue still needs to review outputs and decide whether to create revision tasks; this preserves human/lead control over shared project state.

coordination_policy.specialist_role: execute only the assigned scientific subtask
coordination_policy.shared_state: state-changing PaperGraph/project updates should be gated by lead/synthesis tasks
